to do that. All right, so welcome to Storytime. It is Sunday, July 14th, and I am thrilled to be here with all of you. Storytime is a once a month opportunity for you to sort of get a peek into how I craft stories for the stage. I take a moment from my homework for life over the course of the last 72 hours. Hopefully something happens that is story worthy enough to actually turn into a story. And then I put it together for you here. I think it's important to know that if you don't know what homework for life is, go check out the TED Talk or go to storyworthy.com and learn about it there. It's the most valuable thing that you can be doing as a storyteller or frankly a human being. So if you're not doing homework for life, go figure that out. But if you are doing homework for life, one of the important things I do like to mention is I've done some analysis from my new book, Stories, Sal. People have often asked me how many of the things that you write down actually make it into a story or become a story or make it into your stand up or something you write. And so I did some analysis of two years, the last two years from my book, 10% of what I write down ultimately makes it into either a story or becomes a story itself, which is what's going to happen today, I hope, or it makes it into a comedy bit or a column that I write for a newspaper, all those things. So that means 90% of the things that I write down are actually never heard by anyone except for me and perhaps friends and family. That doesn't mean they have no value. In fact, they're just as valuable to me because I'm holding onto my days, I'm holding into my onto my moments and I never judge anything. So when I write something down, I never judge the value of it at the time. Quite often things that I think are meaningless end up being incredibly important later on because we don't know sort of what story we're in the middle of. And sometimes things that I think are absolutely going to be stories in the moment that they happen. By the dawn of the next day, I realized they're not really stories. My son, Charlie, who is 12, is sitting next to me. And on the other side of Charlie is my wife, Alicia. We love this park, Duncan Donuts Park in the middle of Hartford. We're watching the Hartford Yardgoats play against the Portland Sea dogs. These are teams that we don't care about in any way whatsoever, except we come to the stadium and we watch this team that is meaningless to us play a game that is meaningful to us. It actually has nine cherries on top because Charlie learned somewhere in the past that if you ask for nine cherries and you're a certain age, they will give you nine cherries. And so he somehow always manages to get nine cherries and I get one. It sort of pisses me off all the time, but he's got nine cherries and ice cream and a little plastic up. person in a whole bunch of ways. He has a weird musical brain that I don't understand. We have a keyboard in the house that I bought so I could learn to play piano and I failed doing so. ## [09:09 - 12:07]

It's instantaneous and absolute. I hear him say it's called childhood. And it's like I'm being punched in the face because he's absolutely right. He's 12 years old and he's a boy. He is supposed to get ice cream all over his damn shirt. That is sort of the platonic ideal of a 12 year old boy. This is the thing they're supposed to do. They're supposed to leave their shoes all over the damn place. They're supposed to forget their lunchbox in their backpack all the damn time. And he has reminded me in this moment, this is childhood idiot. This is what I'm supposed to do. The reason I sort of love storytelling is I'm burdened with facts. And that's a phrase I often use when I make up a story, when I write a novel, I can just make up anything. So I'm not burdened by anything. I get to imagine whatever I want. I actually create exceptionally challenging situations for my characters and then force myself to find their ways out of them without changing the situation. My wife loves that. She's like, that's the best thing you do is you get your characters into an impossible situation and then you don't change the situation. You just spend enormous amounts of time trying to figure out how to solve that problem that you've now created. I don't go back and say like, well, I'll just make the problem easier. I never do that. But I like storytelling because I do feel like I'm burdened by a pile of facts. And it's more of a puzzle for me than, than sort of creation. I am choosing which facts I'm going to use. I'm choosing the order. I'm choosing the emphasis and whether I'm going to make them funny or serious, all of those things. But I can't really change the facts to any degree. I can throw facts out of the pile that are not helpful to me. But I will only throw facts out of the pile and not use them if they genuinely help the story. I never throw away something in an effort to make me look better. In fact, that would be the dumbest thing to do. I love facts where I look like an idiot. Those are my favorite facts. But if I think it doesn't help the story, like if I could eliminate my wife from the story, it would make it an easier story for everyone to sort of process. So characters leave all the time. I'm also willing to play with time, meaning if something takes place over the course of three hours, but it's just easier to envision it over the course of 15 minutes, I'll stick it into a 15 minute time block instead of a three hour time block because I don't want to say something happened and then nothing happened for an hour and a half and then something else happened and nothing happened for 20 minutes.
